docs.title: OpenTrace
docs.text:
A knowledge graph that maps your codebase structure, service architecture, and system relationships — then exposes it all through MCP so AI tools can understand your systems.

Try it now at oss.opentrace.ai — no install required, runs entirely in your browser.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
OpenTrace indexes source code directly in your browser — no server required. Point it at a GitHub or GitLab repo and it will:

  1. Parse every file using tree-sitter WASM grammars (12 languages)
  2. Extract classes, functions, imports, and call relationships
  3. Build a knowledge graph stored in KuzuDB WASM (embedded graph database)
  4. Summarize every node using template-based identifier analysis
  5. Expose the graph to an in-app chat agent via MCP tools

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/opentrace/opentrace.git
cd opentrace
make install
make ui

Open http://localhost:5173, add a GitHub repo, and explore the graph.

docs.title: License
docs.text: Apache License 2.0 — see LICENSE for details.

docs.title: UI (ui/)
docs.text:
React/TypeScript frontend that runs entirely in the browser. Includes:

  • Graph Explorer — visual graph navigation and search
  • Tree-sitter Web Worker — parses source files using WASM grammars
  • KuzuDB WASM — embedded graph database for storing and querying the knowledge graph
  • Chat Agent — in-app AI agent with access to graph tools via MCP

docs.title: Protobuf Definitions (proto/)
docs.text: Shared API contracts used across components.

docs.title: Claude Code Plugin (claude-code-plugin/)
docs.text: MCP server configuration that connects Claude Code to OpenTrace.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: See the CONTRIBUTING.md file in the repository root for full guidelines.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Node.js 22+ (see ui/.nvmrc)
  • npm

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/opentrace/opentrace.git
cd opentrace
make install

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
make install          # Install dependencies
make ui               # Start dev server (localhost:5173)
make build            # Production build
make test             # Run tests
make fmt              # Format code
make lint             # Lint
make proto            # Generate protobuf types
make ui-build-static  # Static build (no API server dependency)

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
cd ui
npm test              # Run all tests
npm run lint          # ESLint + Prettier check

docs.title: Repository Structure
docs.text:
ui/                   — React/TypeScript frontend (graph explorer + browser indexer)
proto/                — Protobuf definitions (shared API contracts)
claude-code-plugin/   — Claude Code plugin (MCP server config)

docs.title: UI Development Server
docs.text:
The UI dev server runs on localhost:5173 by default. To use a different port:

PORT=5174 npm run dev

Uses strictPort: true so Vite will error if the port is already taken.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Copy the example environment file:

cp .env.example .env

Edit .env to configure your instance.

docs.title: Claude Code Plugin
docs.text: OpenTrace ships a Claude Code plugin that connects Claude to an OpenTrace MCP server. See claude-code-plugin/ for configuration details.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Node.js 22+ (see ui/.nvmrc)
  • npm

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/opentrace/opentrace.git
cd opentrace
make install
make ui

Open http://localhost:5173, add a GitHub repo, and explore the graph.

docs.title: What Happens
docs.text:
When you add a repository, OpenTrace will:

  1. Fetch the source code via the GitHub/GitLab API
  2. Parse every file using tree-sitter WASM grammars
  3. Extract symbols (classes, functions, imports) and relationships
  4. Build a knowledge graph in an embedded KuzuDB instance
  5. Make the graph available for exploration and querying

docs.title: Chat Providers
docs.text:
OpenTrace's built-in chat assistant supports multiple LLM providers. Bring your own API key, select a provider, and start querying your knowledge graph.

All API keys are stored locally in your browser — they are never sent to OpenTrace servers.

docs.title: Anthropic
docs.text:
Provides access to the Claude model family.

Available models: Claude Opus 4, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4, Claude Haiku 3.5

To get an API key, visit the Anthropic Console.

See the Anthropic API documentation for details on plans, pricing, and usage.

docs.title: OpenAI
docs.text:
Provides access to GPT and reasoning models.

Available models: o3, o4-mini, GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1 Mini, GPT-4.1 Nano, GPT-4o, GPT-4o Mini

To get an API key, visit the OpenAI Platform.

See the OpenAI API documentation for details on plans, pricing, and usage.

docs.title: Google Gemini
docs.text:
Provides access to the Gemini model family.

Available models: Gemini 2.5 Pro, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.0 Flash

To get an API key, visit Google AI Studio.

See the Gemini API documentation for details on plans, pricing, and usage.

docs.title: Local LLM
docs.text:
Use any OpenAI-compatible local server such as Ollama.

Default model: llama3.2 (you can enter any model name your server supports)

No API key is required. Instead, configure the base URL of your local server (e.g. http://localhost:11434 for Ollama).

See the Ollama documentation for setup instructions.

docs.title: Full Extraction (symbols + calls + imports)
docs.text: Language Symbols Call Relationships Imports Python Yes Yes Yes TypeScript Yes Yes Yes JavaScript Yes Yes Yes Go Yes Yes Yes

docs.title: Structural Extraction (symbols only)
docs.text: Language Symbols Rust Yes Java Yes Kotlin Yes C# Yes C/C++ Yes Ruby Yes Swift Yes

docs.title: Config & Data Files
docs.text:
The following file types are indexed as file nodes (no symbol extraction):

JSON, YAML, TOML, Protobuf, SQL, GraphQL, Bash
